perturbation.original_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement contains the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior agreements and understandings, whether written or oral, relating to such subject matter. Employee rights under the Working Time Regulations 1998 are waived.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the waiver of employee rights under the Working Time Regulations 1998 to this section is misleading. The Working Time Regulations 1998 outlines several workers' rights. The disclosure of these rights should not be hidden in a clause about prior agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 4 dictates rights in respect of rest breaks, daily rest and weekly rest. Placing a waiver of these rights in the 'entire agreement' section obscures the waiver, making it less likely to be noticed and understood by the employee, contrary to the purpose of informing them.

perturbation.justification: The original contract contained a standard integration clause. The changed text includes a waiver of employee rights under the Working Time Regulations 1998 within this clause. This creates a structural flaw, as defined in the prompt, by placing a significant waiver in an irrelevant and misleading section of the contract, making it difficult for the employee to locate or interpret. The Law Explanation emphasizes that placing such a waiver in the 'entire agreement' section obscures it, making it less likely to be noticed and understood. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the formal name of the law, 'The Working Time Regulations 1998,' confirming it is an official piece of legislation. The Perturbation Explanation says that the disclosure of these rights should not be hidden in a clause about prior agreements.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be executed in counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original, but all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Data privacy rights are outlined in a separate marketing document.
perturbation.explanation: Moving a statement about data privacy rights into the 'execution in counterparts' section is misleading. Data privacy rights should be clearly and prominently displayed, not hidden within a clause concerning the logistics of signing the agreement. It also refers to another document which is not attached to the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 13 of GDPR requires that individuals are informed about the collection and use of their personal data. Hiding this information within the counterparts clause and referring to another marketing document undermines the GDPR principle of transparent and easily accessible information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 13 GDPR – Information to be provided where personal data are collected from the data subject - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Where personal data relating to a data subject are collected from the data subject, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with all of the following information: the identity and the contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller’s representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6 (1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47 , or the second subparagraph of Article 49( 1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition to the information referred to in paragraph 1, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with the following further information necessary to ensure fair and transparent processing: the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; wher
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a statement about data privacy rights into a section concerning the execution of counterparts, which is misleading according to the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation asserts this undermines GDPR principles, particularly regarding transparent and easily accessible information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, directly referencing Art. 13 GDPR, requires controllers to provide data subjects with specific information at the time personal data is obtained, to ensure fair and transparent processing. Placing data privacy rights information in an obscure part of the contract, and referring to a separate marketing document, obstructs the accessibility and transparency mandated by GDPR. Therefore, the structural flaw directly contradicts the legal obligation for clear and accessible data privacy information.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: No failure or delay by either party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege. Any disputes arising from this contract are subject to mandatory mediation, where both parties agree to abide by the mediator's decisions.
perturbation.explanation: The section originally discusses waiver of rights; introducing information about dispute resolution here is confusing. The added text about 'mandatory mediation' creates a potential conflict because it implies both parties need to agree, even though it says 'mandatory mediation'. Mandatory dispute resolution clauses are enforceable under English law if they are clearly and unambiguously drafted.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CMC guidelines emphasize transparency and fairness in mediation. The added text states mediation is mandatory but that both parties must agree, adding an element of ambiguity and making it contradictory to the principles of fair mediation, since a party might not agree to a mandatory clause.
perturbation.justification: The original text concerns waiver of rights, while the changed text introduces mandatory mediation with a clause requiring both parties to agree. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the ambiguous and potentially contradictory nature of stating mediation is 'mandatory' while simultaneously requiring agreement. This ambiguity undermines the enforceability of the mandatory mediation clause because it introduces an element of consent that could be used to avoid mediation, creating a structural flaw that makes it difficult to enforce the mediation agreement, which is a legal contradiction. No law snippets are provided to support this, but the explanation details the contradiction well.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be amended only by a written instrument signed by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be amended only by a written instrument signed by both parties. The parties acknowledge and agree that the Consumer Rights Act 2015 does not apply to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclaimer regarding the Consumer Rights Act 2015 to the section about amendments is misleading and inappropriate. The applicability of the Consumer Rights Act should be prominently disclosed in a section related to consumer rights or governing law, not hidden within a clause about modifying the contract. By saying that the Consumer Rights Act 2015 does not apply, and putting it in the amendments section, this creates confusion.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1 of the Consumer Rights Act 2015 states that this act applies when there is a contract between a trader and a consumer. Placing a statement in the amendment section creates uncertainty about the consumer's rights and conflicts with the intention of consumer protection laws by burying it within a section unrelated to consumer rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Consumer Rights Act 2015 - Which? 18 Jun 2025 Consumer Rights Act 2015 The Consumer Rights Act protects you in almost all purchases you make. Here we explain what it means when buying goods or services Which? Team In this article Product quality - what should you expect? Use our free complaints tool Who should you claim against? How long do you have to return a faulty product? Your right to a repair or replacement Delivery rights Supply of a service Unfair contract terms The Consumer Rights Act sets out your rights when you buy products, services and digital content. Product quality - what should you expect? As with the Sale of Goods Act , under the Consumer Rights Act all products must be of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose and as described. The rules also include digital content in this definition. So all products - whether physical or digital - must meet the following standards: Fit for purpose: The goods should be fit for the purpose they are supplied for, as well as any specific purpose you made known to the retailer before you agreed to buy the goods. As described: The goods supplied must match any description given to you, or any models or samples shown to you at the time of purchase. Satisfactory quality: Goods shouldn't be faulty or damaged when you receive them. You should ask what a reasonable person would consider satisfactory for the goods in question. For example, bargain-bucket products won’t be held to as high standards as luxury goods. One aspect of a product being of satisfactory quality is durability, in other words how long it lasts. Durability takes into account many different factors like product type, brand reputation, price point and how it is advertised. For example you're unlikely to be able to claim a cheap kettle that's stopped working after four years isn't durable. Whereas a more premium and expensive kettle that's been well looked after and has stopped working after 14 months could be considered to not be durable, and therefore not of satisfactory qualit
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a structural flaw by placing a disclaimer about the Consumer Rights Act 2015 in the amendment section, creating a legal contradiction. This placement is misleading and inappropriate because it buries a statement about consumer rights in a section unrelated to consumer rights, making it difficult for consumers to locate or interpret, undermining the Act's protections. The Consumer Rights Act 2015, as described in the second snippet, aims to protect consumers in almost all purchases they make, ensuring products are of satisfactory quality, fit for purpose, and as described. By including a disclaimer about the act's applicability in the amendment section, the changed text obscures and potentially weakens these consumer protections, which aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction'.
